title: practical english usage - Part 73
content:
But only used to can refer to past states. Compare: Sometimes he used to / would bring me little presents without saying why.

for hours.

When she was old, she used to / would sit in the corner talking to herself Used to and would can both refer to repeated actions and events in the past.

8  used      to   and   would experience - I hadn’ t done it before.) I wasn’ t used to driving a big car. (= Driving a big car was a new and difficult not (     I wasn't used to drive a big car?) I didn’ t use to drive a big car. (= Once  I didn’ t drive a big car, but now I do.) Compare: Used + infinitive has a quite different meaning from be used to .. .ing (►  615).

7 used      + infinitive       and   be   used      to   .. .ing Note the pronunciation of used /ju:st/ and use /ju:s/ in this structure.

I used always to be afraid of dogs. (formal, and rare in AmE) I always used to be afraid of dogs. (informal) used is more common in an informal style.

Mid-position adverbials (►  200) can go before or after used. The  position before 5 word    order last year.) not I went to France seven times last year. (     I used to go to France seven  times Section7 Modal    Auxiliary      Verbs grammar •  Section 8  Infinitives, -ing forms and Past Participles participle clauses in the following explanations unless the longer term is needed.

For the sake of simplicity, 'infinitive’  and 'participle' are used for infinitive and Cooked in a slow oven, it will taste delicious. (participle clause) She ran out laughing like a hyena. (participle clause) I'd like to look at those papers again. (infinitive clause) All of these verb forms can begin clauses (►  Section 10).

The  dividing line between participles and adjectives is not always clear, ►    96.

these forms do not have any particular time reference.

The  names ‘ present participle’  and ‘ past participle’  are misleading, because faster than a speeding bullet She ran out laughing.

They  are called 'present participles' when they are used more like verbs or I enjoy skiing.

Smoking is dangerous.

correspond to infinitives in some other languages.

they are used in similar ways to nouns, as subjects or objects. Gerunds may -ing forms have two grammatical names. They  are often called 'gerunds’  when various other uses which  are explained in Sections 8-10.

to form tenses: e.g. will write, is speaking, has seen (►    15). They  also have call them 'non-finite'. However, they can be used together with auxiliary verbs have any time reference (unlike for example writes or spoke), and grammarians speaking (-ing form) and seen (past participle).
